episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Indigenous-led futures in the north and New Arctic Observing Summit Report Calls for Indigenous-Led Future in , plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Is 'Indigenous-led' project delivery without underlying governance authority a meaningful step toward Indigenous self-determination, or does it obscure persistent authority gaps? Topics include: The distinction between Indigenous-led delivery, design, and governance authority, Post-wildfire forest rehabilitation at Palmer Lake as a case study in governance gaps, Data ownership and control as the next frontier of Indigenous authority, Funding models: grants vs. revenue generation vs. regulatory necessity, Standing institutional capacity as prerequisite to effective negotiation.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

articles.title: [TechRadar] AI exposes the M&A integration gaps that governance must fix
articles.author: https://www.techradar.com/author/murali-thiagarajan
articles.summary: Nine in ten companies now use AI, but M&A keeps revealing what’s broken underneath.

discussion.central_question: Is 'Indigenous-led' project delivery without underlying governance authority a meaningful step toward Indigenous self-determination, or does it obscure persistent authority gaps?
discussion.riley_position: The gap between legal Aboriginal title and actual decision-making authority on the ground remains substantial; projects like Palmer Lake demonstrate this by showing Indigenous-led delivery that still operates within provincial decision-making frameworks.
riley_key_evidence: Tŝilhqot'in title decision (2014) established decision-making authority, but 12 years later the gap between law and ground-level practice at Palmer Lake remains substantial
BC's expanded First Nations-led addictions treatment is Indigenous-led delivery within a provincially funded framework, not Indigenous governance authority
Arctic Observing Summit report recommends funding Indigenous organizations that hold governance authority over data, not just those participating in data collection
discussion.casey_position: The real question is 'who can say no?' — governance authority must be established before programs launch, not negotiated afterward; Indigenous-led delivery is cosmetic unless the Nation retains final decision-making power.
casey_key_evidence: Denisiqi Services Society's 20+ years of operational continuity was built because communities insisted on community-controlled governance, not because the funding model was designed to produce it

At Palmer Lake, urgent provincial needs for slope stabilization and erosion control create conditions where substantive decisions (species selection, timeline, endpoints) remain with the Ministry of Forests despite Indigenous project leadership

Nations need standing legal and technical capacity before negotiation starts, not as project line items; the pattern differentiating stronger outcomes isn't funding size but pre-existing institutional capacity
discussion.resolution: Both agreed that governance authority must precede program delivery and that 'Indigenous-led' covers three distinct things (delivery, design, governance) often conflated. They aligned on moving from defensive veto power to generative standards-setting as a structural model for authority, with Casey proposing data revenue models and certification frameworks as self-sustaining alternatives to grant dependency.
topics_covered: The distinction between Indigenous-led delivery, design, and governance authority
Post-wildfire forest rehabilitation at Palmer Lake as a case study in governance gaps
Data ownership and control as the next frontier of Indigenous authority
Funding models: grants vs. revenue generation vs. regulatory necessity
Standing institutional capacity as prerequisite to effective negotiation
